checks.a_analysis: The content in the JSON follows the sequence of the source document (from page 11 onwards). While large sections of data are flattened into paragraphs or single table cells, there is no evidence of the same text block being repeated more times in the output than it appears in the source.

The JSON metadata identifies 0 headings. The source document contains numerous logical section headers (e.g., 'ArcGIS Desktop and Extensions' on page 2, 'ArcGIS Enterprise' on page 6, 'ArcGIS Online...' on page 30). These headers are either omitted entirely or misidentified as data rows within tables. This results in a completely flat hierarchy which is a major defect for accessibility.
checks.b_evidence_source: Logical section headers like 'ArcGIS Desktop and Extensions' (Page 2) or 'ArcGIS Enterprise' (Page 6).

checks.a_analysis: The table structure is severely corrupted in several ways. First, multi-column tables are rendered as single-column tables with all data merged into a single cell (e.g., Section 1, Table index 0). Second, large portions of the source document containing tables (Pages 23-32) are converted into long paragraphs, losing all structural relationships between part numbers and prices. Third, Section 2 Table index 11 merges data from multiple pages (32, 33, 34) into a single row/cell. Additionally, Section 2 contains fabricated elements like 'Dummy Table for Row Count' (elements 3-10) not present in the source.
checks.b_evidence_source: Tables on pages 11-38 with distinct columns for Part Number, Description, and Prices.
checks.b_evidence_output: Section 1, Table index 0, Row 0: content merged into one cell with colspan 5. Section 2, element index 1: 10 pages of table data flattened into a single paragraph.
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 1, Table index 0; Section 2, elements 1 and 11.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order is fundamentally broken because the first 11 pages of the source document are entirely missing from their correct position. The JSON starts with content from Page 11 at element index 0. While Section 3 contains a title indicating these pages were 'unavailable', the main document flow is critically incomplete and begins mid-way through.
checks.b_evidence_source: Document starts on Page 1 with 'ArcGIS Desktop and Extensions'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The output begins with content from Page 11 ('154823 ArcGIS GIS Server...').

checks.a_analysis: The source document consists almost exclusively of tables. There are no significant list structures to evaluate.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: The provided json_heading_count is 0 and the html_heading_count is 3. The mismatch is exactly 3. According to the rules, a fail occurs only if the mismatch is MORE THAN 3 headings (or 10%). Thus, this check passes.

The source document contains no URLs or clickable links. The JSON output correctly contains no link elements. While the HTML contains 1 link (likely an injected skip link or file link), there is no evidence of fabricated links within the main text content.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the HTML output is significantly different from the source PDF. The source PDF is structured as a series of distinct tables with headers and footers per page. The HTML output first presents a massive, concatenated list of all data points as plain text lines, then follows this with a 'Summary Table Check' section that repeats the data in table format, and concludes with a list of navigation links. This rearrangement confuses the visual flow and makes it difficult to find information in a manner consistent with the source document.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF (e.g., Pages 2-38) presents pricing data in structured tables organized by functional sections (ArcGIS Desktop, ArcGIS Enterprise, etc.).
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows a continuous text dump at the top ('ArcGIS Enterprise Pricing Document'), followed by a 'Summary Table Check' section containing tables, and a long list of internal links to tables at the bottom.
checks.b_evidence_location: Entire document layout as seen in the long HTML screenshot.

checks.a_analysis: The majority of the tabular content from the source PDF is rendered in the main section of the HTML as plain text strings rather than structured HTML tables. While headers are present, the data rows lack clear column boundaries and consistent alignment, making them appear as paragraphs or lines of text. Although some tables are rendered correctly with borders in the 'Summary Table Check' section, the primary content area fails this check.
checks.b_evidence_source: PDF Page 7 contains a 5-column table for 'ArcGIS GIS Server - Perpetual License Prices'.
checks.b_evidence_output: In the HTML screenshot, the same content (e.g., for Part 160483) is rendered as a single line of text without tabular columns: '160483 ArcGIS GIS Server Advanced Up to Four Cores Perpetual License $37,753.00 $38,907.00'.
checks.b_evidence_location: Main content section of the HTML screenshot.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF document consists almost entirely of text-based tables. There are no meaningful logos, charts, or diagrams present in the source material that would require image rendering in the HTML.

The content is visually duplicated throughout the HTML document. The entire set of pricing data is first listed as text lines and then repeated in the 'Summary Table Check' tables. This duplication is not present in the source PDF and significantly degrades the user experience by providing the same information multiple times in different formats on a single page.
checks.b_evidence_source: Each pricing item (e.g., Part 154097) appears exactly once in its respective table in the PDF.
checks.b_evidence_output: Part 154097 appears once in the text list at the top of the HTML screenshot and again within 'Table 22' in the 'Summary Table Check' section.
checks.b_evidence_location: HTML screenshot, seen in both the 'ArcGIS Enterprise Pricing Document' and 'Summary Table Check' sections.

checks.a_analysis: The overall visual quality is poor due to the inconsistent formatting (mixing plain text rows and tables) and the inclusion of redundant text artifacts like repeated page labels ('Page 2 of 39', 'Page 3 of 39', etc.) within the main content stream. The large gaps between elements in the navigation section at the bottom also contribute to a lack of polish.
checks.b_evidence_source: The PDF is a professionally formatted government price list with consistent table styling.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML includes raw page header text mixed with data and inconsistent row rendering styles (plain text vs. bordered tables).
